hook.text: Water takes much longer to heat up than sand. That's why beaches are scorching but the ocean stays cool.

buildup.text: Specific heat capacity measures how much energy is needed to raise one kilogram by one degree.

discovery.text: Water's specific heat is 4,186 J/kg°C—one of the highest of any common substance.

twist.text: This is why coastal cities have milder climates. The ocean absorbs and releases heat slowly, stabilizing temperatures.

climax.text: Car engines use water-based coolant specifically because it absorbs enormous amounts of heat before warming up.

punchline.text: Water resists temperature change. That shapes weather and keeps engines cool.

quiz.question: Why do coastal cities have milder climates?
options: Water's high specific heat absorbs and releases heat slowly
Ocean winds blow away excess heat
Salt in seawater lowers its temperature
